  alertmanagers:
    - static_configs:
        - targets:
            - alertmanager:9093

instruction: How do you provision Grafana contact points and notification policies via config files?
output:
Grafana Unified Alerting supports provisioning contact points and notification policies via YAML files.

# /etc/grafana/provisioning/alerting/contact-points.yaml
apiVersion: 1
contactPoints:
  - orgId: 1
    name: slack-ops
    receivers:
      - uid: slack-ops-uid
        type: slack
        settings:
          url: 'https://hooks.slack.com/services/CHANGEME'
          recipient: '#alerts'
          title: '{{ template "slack.grafana.title" . }}'
          text: '{{ template "slack.grafana.text" . }}'
        disableResolveMessage: false

  - orgId: 1
    name: pagerduty-critical
    receivers:
      - uid: pd-critical-uid
        type: pagerduty
        settings:
          integrationKey: 'CHANGEME'
        secureSettings:
          integrationKey: 'CHANGEME'

# /etc/grafana/provisioning/alerting/notification-policies.yaml
apiVersion: 1
policies:
  - orgId: 1
    receiver: slack-ops
    group_by: [alertname, grafana_folder]
    group_wait: 30s
    group_interval: 5m
    repeat_interval: 4h
    routes:
      - receiver: pagerduty-critical
        matchers:
          - severity = critical
        group_wait: 10s
        repeat_interval: 1h

Notes:
- Files in provisioning/alerting/ are auto-loaded on startup
- deleteIfNew: true removes resources not present in provisioning files
- secureSettings are for encrypted fields (API keys, passwords)

instruction: Write a complete alertmanager.yml that routes critical alerts to PagerDuty and all others to Slack.
input: PagerDuty routing key: ABC123. Slack webhook: https://hooks.slack.com/services/XYZ. Slack channel: #alerts
output:
global:
  resolve_timeout: 5m

route:
  receiver: slack-default
  group_by: [alertname, cluster]
  group_wait: 30s
  group_interval: 5m
  repeat_interval: 4h
  routes:
    - match:
        severity: critical
      receiver: pagerduty-critical
      group_wait: 10s
      repeat_interval: 1h

receivers:
  - name: slack-default
    slack_configs:
      - api_url: 'https://hooks.slack.com/services/XYZ'
        channel: '#alerts'
        title: '[{{ .Status | toUpper }}] {{ .GroupLabels.alertname }}'
        text: '{{ range .Alerts }}{{ .Annotations.description }}{{ end }}'
        send_resolved: true

  - name: pagerduty-critical
    pagerduty_configs:
      - routing_key: ABC123
        description: '{{ .GroupLabels.alertname }}: {{ .CommonAnnotations.summary }}'
        send_resolved: true

inhibit_rules: []

instruction: What is the difference between rate() and irate() in PromQL, and when should you use each?
output:
rate(v range-vector)
  Calculates the per-second AVERAGE rate of increase over the entire range window.
  Handles counter resets (target restarts) automatically.
  Extrapolates to cover the full time range.
  Best for: alerting, slow-moving counters, dashboards with smooth graphs.
  Example: rate(http_requests_total[5m])

irate(v range-vector)
  Calculates the per-second INSTANT rate using only the last two data points.
  Also handles counter resets automatically.
  Much more sensitive to sudden spikes — can be volatile.
  Best for: graphing fast-moving, volatile counters where you need responsiveness.
  Example: irate(http_requests_total[5m])  (window only controls lookback for reset detection)

Key rules:
- Always take rate()/irate() FIRST, then aggregate with sum()/avg()/etc.
  Correct:   sum(rate(http_requests_total[5m])) by (job)
  Incorrect: rate(sum(http_requests_total)[5m])  -- misses counter resets
- Use rate() for alerts — irate's volatility can cause alert flapping
- irate() with a long window like [1h] still only uses the last two points; the window just provides more data for reset detection

instruction: Fix this promtool unit test — it will always pass even when the alert is broken.
input:
rule_files:
  - alerts.yml

tests:
  - interval: 1m
    input_series:
      - series: 'up{job="node"}'
        values: '0 0 0 0 0 0'

    alert_rule_test:
      - eval_time: 10m
        alertname: InstanceDown
output:
The test has a critical flaw: the alert_rule_test case has no exp_alerts field.
When exp_alerts is omitted, promtool does NOT verify whether the alert fires —
it only checks that the alertname is valid.

The test passes whether InstanceDown fires or not — providing no real validation.

Fix — add explicit expectations:

1. Test that alert FIRES:
   alert_rule_test:
     - eval_time: 10m
       alertname: InstanceDown
       exp_alerts:
         - exp_labels:
             severity: critical
             job: node
           exp_annotations:
             summary: "Instance web01:9100 is down"

2. Test that alert does NOT fire before 'for' elapses:
   alert_rule_test:
     - eval_time: 3m
       alertname: InstanceDown
       exp_alerts: []    # explicitly empty = assert no alerts firing

Note: input series '0 0 0 0 0 0' = up=0 for 6 evaluation intervals.
If InstanceDown has 'for: 5m', it should fire at eval_time 5m or later.

instruction: What PromQL label matchers are available and how do they differ?
output:
Four label matchers are available in PromQL:

= (equals): Selects time series where label exactly equals the value.
  Example: http_requests_total{job="api"}

!= (not equals): Selects time series where label does NOT equal the value.
  Example: http_requests_total{status!="200"}

=~ (regex match): Selects time series where label matches the regex. Always fully anchored (treated as ^pattern$).
  Example: http_requests_total{status=~"5.."}  — matches 500, 501, 502, etc.

!~ (not regex match): Selects time series where label does NOT match the regex.
  Example: http_requests_total{method!~"GET|HEAD"}

Rules:
- Regex uses RE2 syntax
- Matches are fully anchored — env=~"prod" only matches exactly "prod", not "production"
- Vector selectors must have at least one matcher that does not match the empty string
- {job=~".*"} is INVALID because it could match an empty label value
- {job=~".+"} is valid

instruction: What does the resets() function do in PromQL and when should you use it?
output:
resets(v range-vector) returns the number of counter resets within
the given range for each time series.

A counter reset occurs when a counter's value decreases, indicating
the process restarted or crashed.

Should only be used with counters (not gauges).

Examples:

Detect how many times a service restarted in the last hour:
  resets(http_requests_total[1h])

Alert if a service has restarted more than 3 times in 1 hour:
  - alert: ServiceFrequentRestarts
    expr: resets(process_start_time_seconds[1h]) > 3
    for: 0m
    labels: {severity: warning}
    annotations:
      summary: "Service {{ $labels.job }} restarted {{ $value }} times in 1h"

Key difference from rate():
  rate() handles resets automatically (adjusts for them)
  resets() explicitly COUNTS the resets

Use cases:
- Detecting crash-looping services
- Auditing how often a process restarts
- Validating stability after deployments

Common mistake: resets() applied to a gauge.
Gauges legitimately decrease (e.g., memory freed), so resets() on
a gauge will produce misleading results.

instruction: Fix this metric_relabel_config — it will silently do nothing.
input:
metric_relabel_configs:
  - source_labels: [__name__]
    regex: go_gc_duration_seconds
    action: Drop
output:
The action value 'Drop' has a capital D — all action values must be lowercase.
'Drop' is not a recognized action, so Prometheus silently ignores the rule
and all go_gc_duration_seconds metrics are stored as normal.

Corrected:
metric_relabel_configs:
  - source_labels: [__name__]
    regex: go_gc_duration_seconds
    action: drop

Tip: use promtool check config prometheus.yml to validate the config file before reloading.

instruction: Explain the top-level sections of a Prometheus configuration file and what each does.
output:
The Prometheus configuration file (prometheus.yml) has these top-level sections:

1. **global** - Default settings applied to all scrape jobs and rule evaluations:
   - scrape_interval: how often to scrape targets (default 1m)
   - scrape_timeout: max time for a scrape (default 10s, must be <= scrape_interval)
   - evaluation_interval: how often to evaluate alerting/recording rules (default 1m)
   - external_labels: labels added to all metrics/alerts sent to external systems

2. **alerting** - Configures Alertmanager endpoints Prometheus sends alerts to.

3. **rule_files** - List of glob patterns for rule files (recording and alerting rules).

4. **scrape_configs** - List of jobs defining what targets to scrape and how.

5. **remote_write** - Optional; sends metrics to remote storage (e.g., Thanos, Cortex).

6. **remote_read** - Optional; reads historical data from remote storage.

instruction: Fix this alertmanager.yml route — it references a receiver that is not defined.
input:
route:
  receiver: slack-ops
  routes:
    - match:
        severity: critical
      receiver: pagerduty-critical

receivers:
  - name: slack-ops
    slack_configs:
      - api_url: 'https://hooks.slack.com/services/XYZ'
        channel: '#alerts'
output:
The route for severity=critical references receiver 'pagerduty-critical',
but no receiver with that name is defined in the receivers list.

Alertmanager will fail to start with:
  'undefined receiver "pagerduty-critical" used in route'

Fix: add the missing receiver definition:
receivers:
  - name: slack-ops
    slack_configs:
      - api_url: 'https://hooks.slack.com/services/XYZ'
        channel: '#alerts'

  - name: pagerduty-critical
    pagerduty_configs:
      - routing_key: 'YOUR_PAGERDUTY_ROUTING_KEY'
        send_resolved: true

instruction: Fix this recording rule — it violates naming conventions and has a wrong expr.
input:
groups:
  - name: recordings
    rules:
      - record: requestRate
        expr: rate(http_requests_total[5m])
output:
Two issues:
1. Recording rule name 'requestRate' violates the recommended naming convention.
   Convention: level:metric:operations
   - level: the aggregation scope (job, instance, cluster, etc.)
   - metric: the source metric name
   - operations: transformations applied

2. The expr lacks a by() clause, so the result retains ALL labels from the source
   metric. This defeats the purpose of pre-aggregation.

Corrected (aggregated by job):
      - record: job:http_requests_total:rate5m
        expr: sum by (job) (rate(http_requests_total[5m]))

Or if per-instance granularity is needed:
      - record: instance:http_requests_total:rate5m
        expr: rate(http_requests_total[5m])

instruction: Fix this Grafana datasource provisioning file — it has two errors.
input:
apiVersion: 1
datasources:
  - name: Prometheus
    type: prometheus
    url: http://prometheus:9090
    access: browser
    jsonData:
      timeInterval: 15
      httpMethod: post
output:
Three errors:
1. access: 'browser' is not a valid value. Valid values are:
   - 'proxy' (recommended) — Grafana server makes requests to the datasource
   - 'direct' — browser makes requests directly (deprecated for most setups)
   The old term 'browser' was renamed to 'direct' and is no longer accepted.

2. timeInterval must be a string with a unit suffix, not a bare integer.
   '15' should be '15s'.

3. httpMethod should be uppercase: 'POST' not 'post'.

Corrected:
apiVersion: 1
datasources:
  - name: Prometheus
    type: prometheus
    url: http://prometheus:9090
    access: proxy
    jsonData:
      timeInterval: 15s
      httpMethod: POST

instruction: The textfile collector in node_exporter is not picking up new .prom files. How do you fix it?
input: Files exist in /var/lib/node_exporter/textfile_collector/ but metrics don't appear in Prometheus.
output:
Textfile collector issues — check in order:

1. Verify the textfile collector is enabled and pointed at the right directory:
   ps aux | grep node_exporter | grep textfile
   Expected: --collector.textfile.directory=/var/lib/node_exporter/textfile_collector
   If missing: add the flag and restart node_exporter.

2. Check file permissions:
   ls -la /var/lib/node_exporter/textfile_collector/
   node_exporter process must have read permission.
   Fix: chmod 644 /var/lib/node_exporter/textfile_collector/*.prom
        chown node_exporter:node_exporter /var/lib/node_exporter/textfile_collector/

3. Check file extension — must be exactly .prom:
   Files named .txt, .metrics, .prometheus are silently ignored.
   Rename: mv backup.txt backup.prom

4. Check file content validity:
   journalctl -u node_exporter | grep -i 'textfile|error|parse'
   Validate manually:
   promtool check metrics < /var/lib/node_exporter/textfile_collector/backup.prom

5. Check for duplicate metric names across files:
   Each metric name can only appear once across all .prom files.
   Duplicates cause node_exporter to log an error and skip ALL files.

6. Verify the metric appears in raw output:
   curl http://localhost:9100/metrics | grep backup
   If the metric is there but not in Prometheus, check the scrape config.

instruction: How do you reload Prometheus configuration without restarting the process?
output:
Two methods to reload Prometheus configuration at runtime:

Method 1 — SIGHUP:
  kill -HUP <prometheus_pid>
  or: pkill -HUP prometheus

Method 2 — HTTP POST (requires --web.enable-lifecycle flag at startup):
  curl -X POST http://localhost:9090/-/reload

The reload:
- Re-reads prometheus.yml and all referenced rule files
- If the new config is invalid (YAML parse error or validation failure), the reload is rejected and the old config stays active
- Prometheus logs will show 'Loading configuration file' and any errors

To verify reload succeeded: check Prometheus logs for 'Completed loading of configuration file' or query the /api/v1/status/config endpoint.

instruction: Write a PromQL query to calculate the 5-minute HTTP error rate as a percentage of total requests, broken down by service.
input: Metrics available: http_requests_total with labels: service, status_code
output:
100 * (
  sum by (service) (
    rate(http_requests_total{status_code=~"5.."}[5m])
  )
  /
  sum by (service) (
    rate(http_requests_total[5m])
  )
)

This calculates, for each service:
- Numerator: per-second rate of 5xx responses over 5 minutes
- Denominator: per-second rate of all requests over 5 minutes
- Multiplied by 100 to get a percentage

If total requests could be zero, use or on() vector(0) to avoid division by zero:
  sum by (service) (rate(http_requests_total{status_code=~"5.."}[5m]))
  /
  (sum by (service) (rate(http_requests_total[5m])) > 0)

instruction: How do you set up windows_exporter to monitor Windows servers?
output:
windows_exporter (github.com/prometheus-community/windows_exporter)
Default port: 9182

1. Install (MSI):
   msiexec /i windows_exporter-0.30.0-amd64.msi \
     ENABLED_COLLECTORS="cpu,cs,logical_disk,memory,net,os,process,service,system"

   Or run directly:
   windows_exporter.exe --collectors.enabled="cpu,cs,logical_disk,memory,net,os,service"

2. prometheus.yml:
   - job_name: windows
     static_configs:
       - targets: ['win-server-01:9182', 'win-server-02:9182']
         labels:
           os: windows

3. Key metrics by collector:

   cpu:
     windows_cpu_time_total{core,mode}        — CPU time by mode

   logical_disk:
     windows_logical_disk_size_bytes{volume}
     windows_logical_disk_free_bytes{volume}
     windows_logical_disk_read_bytes_total{volume}
     windows_logical_disk_write_bytes_total{volume}

   memory:
     windows_memory_available_bytes
     windows_memory_commit_limit

   net:
     windows_net_bytes_received_total{nic}
     windows_net_bytes_sent_total{nic}

   service:
     windows_service_state{name,state}         — running/stopped/etc
     windows_service_start_mode{name}          — automatic/manual/disabled

   os:
     windows_os_info{version,build}            — OS version as labels

4. Key alerts:
   - alert: WindowsServiceDown
     expr: windows_service_state{name="sshd",state="running"} == 0
     for: 5m
     labels: {severity: critical}

   - alert: WindowsLowDiskSpace
     expr: windows_logical_disk_free_bytes / windows_logical_disk_size_bytes < 0.10
     for: 10m

instruction: How do you use the Alertmanager API to manage silences programmatically?
output:
Alertmanager REST API v2 at /api/v2/

CREATE silence:
  curl -X POST http://alertmanager:9093/api/v2/silences \
    -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
    -d '{
      "matchers": [
        {"name": "alertname", "value": "InstanceDown", "isRegex": false, "isEqual": true},
        {"name": "instance",  "value": "web01:9100",   "isRegex": false, "isEqual": true}
      ],
      "startsAt": "2026-05-30T02:00:00Z",
      "endsAt":   "2026-05-30T05:00:00Z",
      "createdBy": "oncall-operator",
      "comment":   "Planned maintenance"
    }'
  Response: {"silenceID": "abc123-..."}

LIST silences:
  curl http://alertmanager:9093/api/v2/silences
  # Filter: ?filter=alertname%3D%22InstanceDown%22

EXPIRE silence early:
  curl -X DELETE http://alertmanager:9093/api/v2/silences/abc123-...

LIST current alerts:
  curl http://alertmanager:9093/api/v2/alerts
  curl 'http://alertmanager:9093/api/v2/alerts?filter=severity%3D%22critical%22'

LIST alert groups:
  curl http://alertmanager:9093/api/v2/alerts/groups

CLUSTER STATUS:
  curl http://alertmanager:9093/api/v2/status

Python automation:
  import requests, datetime

  def silence(instance, hours=2):
      now = datetime.datetime.utcnow()
      end = now + datetime.timedelta(hours=hours)
      r = requests.post('http://alertmanager:9093/api/v2/silences', json={
          'matchers': [{'name': 'instance', 'value': instance,
                        'isRegex': False, 'isEqual': True}],
          'startsAt': now.strftime('%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ'),
          'endsAt':   end.strftime('%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ'),
          'createdBy': 'automation',
          'comment': f'Maintenance for {instance}'
      })
      return r.json()['silenceID']

instruction: Write a complete Alertmanager Slack notification template file with rich formatting.
Explain the avg_over_time(), max_over_time(), and min_over_time() functions with examples.
output:
The _over_time() family aggregates across time within a range vector, returning an instant vector.
These work on GAUGE metrics; for counters use rate() or increase() instead.

avg_over_time(v range-vector)
  Average of all sample values in the range.
  Example: avg_over_time(node_memory_MemAvailable_bytes[1h])
  Returns average available memory over the last hour.

max_over_time(v range-vector)
  Maximum sample value in the range.
  Use for: peak usage, worst-case analysis.
  Example: max_over_time(node_cpu_utilization[1h])

min_over_time(v range-vector)
  Minimum sample value in the range.
  Example: min_over_time(redis_connected_clients[24h])
  Returns minimum connection count in the last 24 hours.

sum_over_time(v range-vector)
  Sum of all values in the range.
  NOT for standard counters — use increase() instead.
  Use for: incrementing gauge-like metrics.

count_over_time(v range-vector)
  Count of samples in the range.
  Use for: detecting gaps in data collection.
  If count_over_time(metric[5m]) < expected_samples: data may be missing.

last_over_time(v range-vector)
  Most recent sample in the range.
  Use for: filling in missing data points in dashboards.

quantile_over_time(scalar, v range-vector)
  phi-quantile of values over time.
  Example: quantile_over_time(0.99, http_response_time_seconds[1h])
  Returns the p99 of all response time samples over 1 hour.

instruction: Fix this node_exporter systemd service — it will fail to expose filesystem metrics correctly.
input:
[Service]
ExecStart=/usr/local/bin/node_exporter
  --collector.filesystem.ignored-mount-points='^/(sys|proc|dev|host|etc)($|/)'
output:
Two problems:
1. The flag --collector.filesystem.ignored-mount-points is deprecated and was
   removed in node_exporter v1.0. The current flag is:
   --collector.filesystem.mount-points-exclude

2. In a systemd unit file, $ must be escaped as $$ to prevent systemd from
   interpreting it as a unit specifier. Single quotes do not help here.

Corrected:
[Service]
ExecStart=/usr/local/bin/node_exporter \
  --collector.filesystem.mount-points-exclude=^/(sys|proc|dev|host|etc)($$|/)

instruction: How do you monitor container resource usage with cAdvisor in Kubernetes?
output:
cAdvisor is built into the Kubernetes kubelet, exposing container metrics at /metrics/cadvisor on each node.

1. Kubernetes scrape config (via kubelet):
   - job_name: cadvisor
     scheme: https
     tls_config:
       ca_file: /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/ca.crt
       insecure_skip_verify: true
     bearer_token_file: /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/token
     kubernetes_sd_configs:
       - role: node
     relabel_configs:
       - action: labelmap
         regex: __meta_kubernetes_node_label_(.+)
       - target_label: __address__
         replacement: kubernetes.default.svc:443
       - source_labels: [__meta_kubernetes_node_name]
         regex: (.+)
         target_label: __metrics_path__
         replacement: /api/v1/nodes/$1/proxy/metrics/cadvisor

2. Key metrics:
   container_cpu_usage_seconds_total{container,pod,namespace}
   container_memory_working_set_bytes{container,pod,namespace}
   container_memory_rss{container,pod,namespace}
   container_network_receive_bytes_total{interface,pod,namespace}
   container_network_transmit_bytes_total{interface,pod,namespace}
   container_fs_usage_bytes{device,pod,namespace}
   container_fs_limit_bytes{device,pod,namespace}

3. CPU usage as % of request:
   rate(container_cpu_usage_seconds_total{container!=""}[5m])
   / on(pod,namespace) kube_pod_container_resource_requests{resource="cpu"}

4. Key alerts:
   - alert: ContainerHighMemory
     expr: |
       container_memory_working_set_bytes{container!=""}
       / on(pod,namespace) kube_pod_container_resource_limits{resource="memory"}
       > 0.90
     for: 5m
     annotations:
       summary: "Container {{ $labels.container }} in pod {{ $labels.pod }} at {{ $value | humanizePercentage }} memory"

instruction: Prometheus is consuming too much memory and eventually gets OOM-killed. How do you diagnose and reduce memory usage?
input: Prometheus process is killed by OOM killer on a 16GB RAM server after a few days.
output:
OOM in Prometheus is almost always caused by high time series cardinality.

Diagnosis:

1. Check total active time series:
   curl 'http://localhost:9090/api/v1/query?query=prometheus_tsdb_head_series'
   Healthy: < 500K series. Concerning: > 1M series.

2. Find highest-cardinality jobs:
   topk(10, count by (job) ({__name__=~".+"}))

3. Find highest-cardinality metric names:
   topk(10, count by (__name__) ({__name__=~".+"}))

4. Check memory metrics:
   process_resident_memory_bytes{job="prometheus"}
   prometheus_tsdb_head_chunks

Fixes:

1. Drop high-cardinality metrics via metric_relabel_configs:
   metric_relabel_configs:
     - source_labels: [__name__]
       regex: 'go_gc_.*|go_memstats_.*'
       action: drop

2. Blank high-cardinality label values:
   metric_relabel_configs:
     - source_labels: [user_id]
       target_label: user_id
       replacement: ''

3. Reduce retention: --storage.tsdb.retention.time=7d instead of 15d

4. Reduce scrape frequency for noisy jobs: scrape_interval: 60s

5. Use out-of-order ingestion limits (Prometheus 2.39+)

instruction: How do you monitor MySQL with mysqld_exporter? Include setup, key metrics, and alerts.
output:
mysqld_exporter exposes MySQL/MariaDB metrics on port 9104.

1. Create a MySQL monitoring user:
   CREATE USER 'exporter'@'localhost' IDENTIFIED BY 'password' WITH MAX_USER_CONNECTIONS 3;
   GRANT PROCESS, REPLICATION CLIENT, SELECT ON *.* TO 'exporter'@'localhost';

2. Configure credentials (.my.cnf):
   [client]
   user=exporter
   password=password
   host=localhost
   port=3306

3. Start:
   mysqld_exporter --config.my-cnf=/etc/.my.cnf

4. prometheus.yml:
   - job_name: mysql
     static_configs:
       - targets: ['db-server:9104']
         labels:
           role: primary

5. Key metrics:
   mysql_up                                              — 1 if connected
   mysql_global_status_threads_connected                 — current connections
   mysql_global_variables_max_connections                — connection limit
   mysql_global_status_queries                           — query counter
   mysql_global_status_slow_queries                      — slow queries
   mysql_global_status_innodb_buffer_pool_read_requests  — cache hits
   mysql_global_status_innodb_buffer_pool_reads          — cache misses (disk reads)

6. Essential alerts:
   - alert: MySQLDown
     expr: mysql_up == 0
     for: 1m
     labels:
       severity: critical

   - alert: MySQLHighConnections
     expr: mysql_global_status_threads_connected / mysql_global_variables_max_connections > 0.80
     for: 5m
     labels:
       severity: warning
     annotations:
       summary: "MySQL connections at {{ $value | humanizePercentage }} of max on {{ $labels.instance }}"

   - alert: MySQLLowBufferPoolHitRate
     expr: |
       rate(mysql_global_status_innodb_buffer_pool_reads[5m])
       / rate(mysql_global_status_innodb_buffer_pool_read_requests[5m]) > 0.05
     for: 10m
     annotations:
       summary: "MySQL InnoDB buffer pool hit rate below 95% on {{ $labels.instance }}"

instruction: How do you import and export Grafana dashboards via the API?
output:
Grafana provides a full HTTP API for dashboard management.

EXPORT (get dashboard JSON):
  curl -H 'Authorization: Bearer <api-token>' \
    http://grafana:3000/api/dashboards/uid/my-dashboard-uid

  Extract just the dashboard JSON:
  curl -s -H 'Authorization: Bearer <token>' \
    http://grafana:3000/api/dashboards/uid/my-dashboard-uid \
    | jq '.dashboard' > my-dashboard.json

IMPORT (create/update dashboard):
  curl -X POST \
    -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
    -H 'Authorization: Bearer <api-token>' \
    -d '{
      "dashboard": <dashboard JSON>,
      "overwrite": true,
      "folderId": 0,
      "message": "Updated via API"
    }' \
    http://grafana:3000/api/dashboards/db

LIST all dashboards:
  curl -H 'Authorization: Bearer <token>' \
    'http://grafana:3000/api/search?type=dash-db'

FOLDER management:
  GET  /api/folders              — list folders
  POST /api/folders              — create folder
    body: {"title": "Infrastructure", "uid": "infrastructure"}

CREATE service account API token:
  Grafana UI > Administration > Service accounts > Add service account token
  Or via API: POST /api/serviceaccounts/<id>/tokens

Script to export all dashboards:
  for uid in $(curl -s -H 'Authorization: Bearer <token>' \
      'http://grafana:3000/api/search' | jq -r '.[].uid'); do
    curl -s -H 'Authorization: Bearer <token>' \
      "http://grafana:3000/api/dashboards/uid/$uid" \
      | jq '.dashboard' > "${uid}.json"
  done

instruction: Write a Prometheus recording rule that pre-computes the per-job request rate.
input: Source metric: http_requests_total with label: job. Evaluation interval: 1m.
output:
groups:
  - name: http_recording_rules
    interval: 1m
    rules:
      - record: job:http_requests_total:rate5m
        expr: sum by (job) (rate(http_requests_total[5m]))
        labels: {}

Naming follows the convention: level:metric:operations
- level: job (the aggregation level)
- metric: http_requests_total (source metric, often shortened)
- operations: rate5m (applied transformations)

This pre-computed metric can then be queried as job:http_requests_total:rate5m in dashboards and alerts, reducing query time at read time.

instruction: What are the Prometheus storage retention flags and what do they control?
output:
Prometheus storage retention is controlled by CLI flags (not in prometheus.yml):

--storage.tsdb.retention.time (default: 15d)
  How long to keep data. Data older than this is deleted.
  Example: --storage.tsdb.retention.time=30d

--storage.tsdb.retention.size (default: 0, disabled)
  Maximum size of the TSDB storage block. When exceeded, oldest data is deleted.
  Example: --storage.tsdb.retention.size=10GB
  Supported units: B, KB, MB, GB, TB, PB, EB.

Note: when both are set, whichever limit is reached first triggers deletion.

--storage.tsdb.path (default: data/)
  Directory where TSDB data is stored.

--storage.tsdb.min-block-duration / --storage.tsdb.max-block-duration
  Control block compaction timing (advanced, rarely changed).

To check current retention/storage: GET /api/v1/status/tsdb shows block statistics.

instruction: How do you use the Prometheus HTTP API to query metrics programmatically?
output:
Prometheus exposes a REST HTTP API at /api/v1/.

INSTANT QUERY:
  GET /api/v1/query?query=<expr>&time=<timestamp>
  curl 'http://localhost:9090/api/v1/query?query=up'
  curl 'http://localhost:9090/api/v1/query?query=up&time=2026-05-30T00:00:00Z'

RANGE QUERY:
  GET /api/v1/query_range?query=<expr>&start=<t>&end=<t>&step=<duration>
  curl 'http://localhost:9090/api/v1/query_range?query=rate(http_requests_total[5m])&start=2026-05-30T00:00:00Z&end=2026-05-30T01:00:00Z&step=60s'

SERIES LOOKUP:
  GET /api/v1/series?match[]=<selector>
  curl 'http://localhost:9090/api/v1/series?match[]=http_requests_total{job="api"}'

LABEL VALUES:
  GET /api/v1/label/<label_name>/values
  curl 'http://localhost:9090/api/v1/label/job/values'

TARGETS (current state):
  curl 'http://localhost:9090/api/v1/targets'
  curl 'http://localhost:9090/api/v1/targets?state=down'

RULES:
  curl 'http://localhost:9090/api/v1/rules?type=alert'

FIRING ALERTS:
  curl 'http://localhost:9090/api/v1/alerts'

Response format:
  {"status": "success", "data": {"resultType": "vector", "result": [...]}}

Each result item:
  {"metric": {"__name__": "up", "job": "node", "instance": "web01:9100"},
   "value": [1748563200.0, "1"]}   // [unix_timestamp, string_value]

Python example:
  import requests
  r = requests.get('http://localhost:9090/api/v1/query',
                   params={'query': 'up'})
  for series in r.json()['data']['result']:
      print(series['metric'], series['value'][1])

instruction: How do you configure Microsoft Teams as an Alertmanager receiver?
output:
Alertmanager does not have a native Teams receiver, but supports it
via the webhook receiver using a Teams incoming webhook URL.

1. Create a Teams incoming webhook:
   Teams channel > ... > Connectors > Incoming Webhook > Configure
   Copy the webhook URL (https://outlook.office.com/webhook/...)

2. Alertmanager webhook receiver:
receivers:
  - name: teams-ops
    webhook_configs:
      - url: 'https://outlook.office.com/webhook/YOUR_WEBHOOK_URL'
        send_resolved: true

This sends Alertmanager's JSON payload format which Teams accepts as plain text.

3. For rich Teams Adaptive Cards, use the prometheus-msteams adapter:
   # Run adapter
   docker run -d -p 2000:2000 \
     -e 'TEAMS_INCOMING_WEBHOOK_URL=https://outlook.office.com/webhook/...' \
     quay.io/prometheusmsteams/prometheus-msteams

   # Alertmanager points to adapter:
   receivers:
     - name: teams-ops
       webhook_configs:
         - url: 'http://prometheus-msteams:2000/alertmanager'
           send_resolved: true

4. Alternative: Grafana Unified Alerting has native Teams contact point support.
   Type: teams, URL: https://outlook.office.com/webhook/...

Teams webhook URL format:
  https://outlook.office.com/webhook/<tenant-id>@<tenant>/IncomingWebhook/<id>/<key>
New Teams (Power Automate) URL format:
  https://<tenant>.webhook.office.com/webhookb2/<id>/IncomingWebhook/<key>

instruction: Write a Grafana datasource provisioning file for Prometheus.
input: Prometheus URL: http://prometheus:9090. Scrape interval: 15s. Should be the default datasource.
output:
# /etc/grafana/provisioning/datasources/prometheus.yaml
apiVersion: 1

datasources:
  - name: Prometheus
    type: prometheus
    uid: prometheus-main
    url: http://prometheus:9090
    access: proxy
    isDefault: true
    editable: false
    jsonData:
      timeInterval: 15s
      queryTimeout: 60s
      httpMethod: POST
      prometheusType: Prometheus
      prometheusVersion: 3.12.0

Explanation:
- access: proxy — Grafana server makes the request (recommended over 'direct')
- isDefault: true — this datasource is pre-selected in new panels
- editable: false — prevents UI changes (good for IaC/GitOps)
- timeInterval: should match Prometheus scrape_interval to avoid gaps in graphs
- uid: stable identifier used to reference this datasource in dashboard JSON

instruction: Fix this TLS config block — it uses wrong field names.
input:
tls_config:
  certificate: /etc/prometheus/certs/client.crt
  private_key: /etc/prometheus/certs/client.key
  ca_certificate: /etc/prometheus/certs/ca.crt
  skip_verify: true
output:
Four field names are wrong:
1. certificate -> cert_file
2. private_key -> key_file
3. ca_certificate -> ca_file
4. skip_verify -> insecure_skip_verify

Corrected:
tls_config:
  cert_file: /etc/prometheus/certs/client.crt
  key_file: /etc/prometheus/certs/client.key
  ca_file: /etc/prometheus/certs/ca.crt
  insecure_skip_verify: true

Note: insecure_skip_verify: true disables certificate validation and should not be used in production.

instruction: What is MonVisor?
output: MonVisor is an AI-assisted monitoring advisor for Prometheus, Alertmanager, and Grafana. It scans a network to discover and fingerprint services, lets you approve which to monitor, then uses a local RAG knowledge base plus a local Ollama model to generate Prometheus/Alertmanager/rules YAML for the services it found. Everything runs locally on your host — no cloud, no API cost. The typical flow is: init → env add → scan → review → generate.

instruction: How do I set up MonVisor for the first time?
output: Run `monvisor init` once before anything else. It creates the data directory (~/.monvisor/ with state.db, the chroma/ vector store, reports/, and configs/), loads the bundled RAG knowledge base, and sets a password for the web UI. Ollama must be running first, with both an LLM and the embedding model nomic-embed-text pulled, since init builds embeddings. After init, register a network with `monvisor env add`.

instruction: What's the full workflow for MonVisor?
output:
The standard MonVisor workflow is five steps:
1. `monvisor init` — one-time setup.
2. `monvisor env add prod 192.168.1.0/24` — register a network as an environment named 'prod'.
3. `monvisor scan prod` — discover and fingerprint services.
4. `monvisor review prod` — approve which services to monitor.
5. `monvisor generate prod` — build the Prometheus/rules/alert YAML.
Deploying those configs to a server (`monvisor deploy`) is a paid-tier feature and not yet available.

instruction: What is an environment in MonVisor?
output: An environment is a named group of one or more CIDR ranges that you scan and manage together. Create one with `monvisor env add <name> <cidr>` (e.g. `monvisor env add prod 192.168.1.0/24`), add more ranges with `monvisor env add-cidr <name> <cidr>`, and see everything with `monvisor env list`. Most other commands take the environment name as their argument, e.g. `monvisor scan prod`.

instruction: How do I run a scan?
output:
`monvisor scan <env>` scans every CIDR in the environment, fingerprints the services it finds, and writes both a terminal report and an HTML file. Useful flags:
• `--host <target>` — re-scan specific targets only (an IP, a range like 192.168.87.36-40, or a CIDR); repeatable.
• `--new-only` — record only hosts/ports not seen in a previous scan.
• `--no-html` — terminal report only, skip the HTML file.
Scanning uses nmap, so nmap must be installed and on PATH.

instruction: How do I review discovered services?
output: `monvisor review <env>` walks you through each discovered service in the terminal so you can decide what to monitor. For each service you press a key: y (yes, monitor it), n (no, skip), b (blackbox — monitor by remote probe when you can't install an exporter), s (skip for now), a (accept all remaining), q (quit). Approved services are what `monvisor generate` later turns into scrape configs. Prefer a browser? Use `monvisor ui <env>` instead.

instruction: How do I use the web interface?
output: `monvisor ui <env>` launches a local web review UI (FastAPI) as an alternative to terminal review. It listens on 127.0.0.1 port 7373 by default (override with the MONVISOR_PORT environment variable). Log in with the password you set during `monvisor init`. It does the same job as `monvisor review` — approving services for monitoring — just in a browser.

instruction: How do I generate Prometheus configs?
output: `monvisor generate <env>` takes the services you approved during review and produces deployable YAML: a prometheus.yml with scrape jobs for your actual hosts, plus recording/alerting rules and Alertmanager config. It combines RAG retrieval against the knowledge base with a local Ollama model. Output files are written under ~/.monvisor/configs/. Run scan and review first, or there's nothing approved to generate from.

instruction: My scan isn't finding any hosts, what's wrong?
output: If a scan finds no hosts or services, check the basics first: nmap must be installed and on your PATH (scanning depends on it), the host running MonVisor must actually reach the target network, and the CIDR you registered with `monvisor env add` must match where your services live. Firewalls or host-based filtering can also hide ports. Try a narrower `monvisor scan <env> --host <ip>` against one known-good machine to confirm scanning works at all.

instruction: Why does generate fail or hang?
output: `monvisor generate` depends on a local Ollama instance, so most failures trace back to it. Make sure Ollama is running and reachable at its URL (default http://localhost:11434, override with MONVISOR_OLLAMA_URL or `monvisor config set ollama-url`), and that the required models are pulled — both the LLM and the nomic-embed-text embedding model. Slow generation usually means the model is large for your hardware; a smaller model speeds it up. Also confirm you've approved services in review, or there's nothing to generate.

instruction: Can I see an example config without scanning?
output: Two different things. You can ask me for an example config any time — e.g. 'show a Prometheus scrape config for node_exporter' or 'a blackbox HTTP probe' — and I'll give an illustrative, generic snippet from the knowledge base. That's great for learning or hand-editing. To get a config wired to YOUR actual machines (real IPs, ports, and the services MonVisor discovered), run `monvisor generate <env>` after scanning and reviewing — it produces deployable YAML specific to your network. Short version: ask me for examples; run generate for your real, host-specific configs.
